date: 2026-07-06
video_id: 4TfpeRYVYDE
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4TfpeRYVYDE
title: Will the Fed spoil the good times soon?

Happy Monday out there team 42. Your skipper here, Darius Dale, to present our macro minute for Monday, July 6th, 2026. As always, we'll start with the executive summary from today's lead-off morning note. So, let's dive right in. Today's key macro question is, will the Fed spoil the good times soon? The short answer is, US dollar money markets are appropriately pricing in a lower probability of Fed rate hikes because of peak inflation. Risk assets are inappropriately pricing in the risk of tighter Fed balance sheet policy because of sticky inflation. The Fed has effectively concluded its reserve management program for now. The next step is a return to balance sheet run-off. A return to balance sheet run-off will likely ripple through financial markets in a rather negative manner, given the observed shift from an ample to abundant reserves regime to an ample to scarce reserves regime. Late summer early fall represents the highest probability window of vulnerability for pricing in this risk. In terms of an analytical nuance, the June ISM Services PMI and economy-weighted ISM Services Composite PMI data supported the consensus outlook for slowing growth. The June S&P Global Composite PMI data did not. The June ISM Services PMI data supported our resilient US economy theme. It did not support our jobless recovery theme. The June ISM Services PMI data supported the consensus outlook for peaking inflation. The June ISM Services PMI data supported our sticky inflation theme. And finally, the Fed's June 16th-17th minutes arrive on Wednesday. Their significance grew after Fed Chair Kevin Warsh trended both the statement and withheld forward guidance both at the meeting and at the ECB Symmetra conference last week. We are aligned with consensus in expecting the minutes to underscore above-target inflation and signal an openness to further tightening. As always, I'll wrap up with a question from our community. This one's titled, "Why the Warsh Fed is a tailwind for data-led macro models?" It says, "Kevin Warsh has taken away the Fed's narrative, no dot plot, no forward guidance, a policy statement gutted to the facts, and an open shot at the stale frameworks the the leaned on for decades, the Phillips curve first among them. Strip out the guidance and the broken theory, and the only thing left to trade is the data, earnings, productivity, inflation prints, not Fed tea leaves. Mhm, amen. Uh this is the world 42 macro and case was built for. Uh most of the street spent years parsing Fed word choice and positioning around guidance. KISS made a data led uh KISS made data led a discipline reading the real life signal directly with no narrative layered on top and nothing to front run. The Fed just made that approach the default. Everyone else is about to be dragged onto the turf we've been trading from all along. That's the part worth sitting with. The most powerful central bank in the world just adopted the premise KISS was built on. The debate over whether to follow the data or the Fed is over. The Fed just ended it. So, the real question isn't whether KISS was right, it's what happens to the rest of the market when the crowd that front run uh that ran on Fed guidance suddenly has to trade without it. We're already three steps ahead. And so, I I wholeheartedly concur with this, obviously. I'm biased here, so uh you can take my uh concurrence with a grain of salt here. But, the key takeaway here is that what Kevin Warsh is trying to do, which I wholeheartedly support as a proud American patriot, is he's trying to get the markets to wean themselves off of being addicted to Fed speak. You know, what does this person say at today? What's this person going to say at 2:30? What's this person going to say at 4:15? That's ridiculous. That's a ridiculous way to operate global financial markets. Obviously, the Fed has an outsized influence on global financial markets being the world's most important uh central bank. And so, clearly the markets are appropriately focused on them to the extent that they keep having this popcorn of of of of Fed speak. But, if you can rein in that popcorn of Fed speak and force financial market participants to go back to doing what they're supposed to be doing, which is pricing in economic risk as opposed to pricing in what they think the Fed is going to say next about their misinterpretation of economic risk, do you hear me say that? >> [laughter] >> Let me say that again. The markets need to go back to pricing in economic risk as opposed to pricing in what they think the next Fed's person will say in regards to their misinterpretation of economic risk because we know the Fed's terrible forecasting their models have been horribly inaccurate. Their forecast for the policy rate have been horribly inaccurate. The summary of economic projections has been horribly inaccurate. They've been very bad at getting to their 2% inflation target. They're continuing to lie to us about uh their willingness about the 2% inflation target, but that's enough here. I digress. The key takeaway is if you don't understand how the economy and financial markets work, you're going to struggle when the markets are left, you know, without the hand-holding uh and the training wheels associated with every Fed member guiding the markets from a day-to-day perspective to the next, you know, uh Fed members uh Fed speak. And so, you know, getting back into the kind of key takeaway here is you know, we built KISS to be, you know, systematic and a KISS and Dr. Mo, you know, the all KISS is a very modified uh and a narrow version of Dr. Mo. Dr. Mo's 70 factors, KISS is three factors. But, we built KISS and Dr. Mo to be agnostic to the evolving nature of the distribution of probable economic, market, and policy outcomes. And if we're agnostic to that, then we can compress the latency from the time, you know, we have a view and it turns out not to be correct to, you know, to to actually making the appropriate pivots, repositioning our portfolio. Uh and investors who, you know, have a much, you know, investors who aren't data-driven, who aren't used to figuring out what's actually happening in across macro cycles, who all you know, who've been so addicted to Fed speak for such a long time, you know, there's going to be a gigantic hole opening up in their process, you know, that they're going to struggle to fill in our opinion. Uh and so, we think that's going to be one of the key legacies of the Kevin Warsh Fed. And so, if you're not, you know, if you're not someone who doesn't, you know, if you're not someone who can piece together the macro puzzle without Fed speak telling you what the macro puzzle is irrespective of how accurate the Fed speak is or not, if you need the Fed speak to understand what's going on in the economy, then it's highly likely that the latency between you your your your your views becoming wrong and you repositioning your portfolio to get on the right side of market risk, that latency is going to widen. And the widening latency is going to cost a lot of investors a lot of performance in the coming quarters and years in our opinion. So, it definitely behooves you to partner with somebody who can compress that latency both from a research perspective, but all and most importantly from a risk management perspective and that's exactly what Kison and I do at Macro Four. So, we'll wrap it up there. Darius here presenting our Macro Minute for Monday, July 6th, 2026. Everyone have a wonderful day out there. Best of luck. We'll catch you back here tomorrow. Cheers.
